title: Pakistani Palak Gosht

ingredients.name: fresh spinach, washed and roughly chopped

subs.name: 500 g frozen spinach, thawed and squeezed dry
subs.notes: add directly without extra cooking time

steps.text: Heat oil in a wide, heavy pot over high heat. Add cumin seeds and let them splutter. Add the chopped onion and fry on medium-high heat for 8–10 minutes, stirring often, until it turns a rich, even golden-brown.

Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 2 minutes until fragrant and the raw smell has gone. Add the chopped tomatoes, red chilli powder, turmeric, coriander powder, and salt. Stir and cook for 5 minutes until the tomatoes break down into a paste.

Add the mutton pieces and sear on high heat for 5 minutes, turning once, until the outer surfaces are browned and sealed. Pour in 200 ml hot water, cover, and cook on low heat for 40 minutes. Check and stir every 10 minutes, adding a splash of water if the masala is sticking.

Add the fresh spinach and green chillies directly on top of the tender mutton. Cover and cook on medium heat for 5 minutes — the spinach will collapse dramatically in volume. Stir everything together thoroughly and cook uncovered for 3 more minutes.

For a smoother gravy, use the back of the spatula to mash some of the spinach against the pot wall, or give a quick partial blend with a hand blender for a creamier texture. Taste for salt and add garam masala.
Finish with a swirl of cream if using. Garnish with ginger julienne and serve with hot naan or chapati.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: mutton → 600 g chicken thighs (bone-in) (reduce braising time to 20 minutes); spinach → 500 g frozen spinach, thawed and squeezed dry (add directly without extra cooking time); cream → 2 tbsp full-fat milk (lighter finish)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: This recipe is essentially identical to north Indian palak gosht and needs no adaptation — it cooks well in any kadai or pressure cooker. For a pressure cooker shortcut, cook the mutton masala for 5 whistles on medium heat, open naturally, then add the spinach and cook uncovered for 5 minutes on medium heat. Fresh palak (spinach) from Indian markets is identical to that used in Pakistan.
